coredata.dc:title: A self-assembled theranostic nanoplatform for efficient tumor sonodynamic-gas therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Shasha
Gao, Zhen
Wei, Fengyuan
Ye, Xueli
Mou, Juan
Yang, Shiping
Wu, Huixia
coredata.dc:description: The powerful antioxidant system in tumor tissues limits the efficacy of sonodynamic therapy (SDT) by reducing the availability of the produced reactive oxygen species (ROS). To address this problem, a pH/ultrasound (US) responsive theranostic nanoplatform is elaborately constructed by self-assembly of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), benzothiazole sulfinate (BTS), Gd3+ ions and poly(lactide-co-glycolide)-b-poly(ethylene glycol) (PLGA-PEG). The NGQD component with excellent sonodynamic activity wrapped in the resulting Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can efficaciously respond to ultrasonic irradiation and cause abundant ROS generation. Importantly, in response to acidic tumor microenvironment, the loaded BTS sustainably releases SO2, which induces the increase of intracellular •O2 − level by inhibiting superoxide dismutase activity and further promotes the enrichment of ROS by reducing intracellular glutathione level and suppressing glutathione peroxidase 4 activity. The well-designed SO2 gas therapy-enhanced SDT strategy leads to high oxidative stress in tumor cells and effective inhibition of the growth of bilateral tumors with only a single intravenous injection of the nanomedicine and a subsequent single US irradiation. Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can also be employed as a good T 1-weighted magnetic resonance imaging contrast agent to guide tumor therapy. This innovative therapeutic strategy developed in our contribution may provide a new paradigm for the exploration of deep tumor therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural and compositional characterizations. (a) TEM image of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. (b) Zeta potentials of NGQDs, NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG dispersed in water, respectively. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of NGQDs, BTS, PLGA-PEG, NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. (d) FTIR spectra of NGQDs, BTS, PLGA-PEG, NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. (e) XPS survey spectrum of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. (f) High-resolution Gd 4d spectrum. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vitro characterizations of 1O2 generation capacity and SO2 release ability. (a) FL spectra of ABDA under the condition of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + ABDA + US. Normalized FL spectra of DEACA incubated with Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG under different conditions: (b) pH = 6.5 and (c) pH = 5.2. (d) SO2 release curves of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG at different pH values. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vitro and in vivo MRI performance. (a) The linear regression fit of 1/T 1 and 1/T 2 versus Gd concentration for Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. (b) T 1, T 2-weighted MRI images for Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG with different Gd concentrations. T 1-weighted MRI images of unilateral tumor-bearing mice (c) and bilateral tumor-bearing mice (d) before (0 h) and after (3, 6, 9 and 12 h) injection of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. (e) The change in relative T 1 signal (The signal of tumor divided by that of muscle) of unilateral tumor. (f) The change in relative T 1 signal of bilateral tumor: (f) left site, (g) right site. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Viability of 4T1 cells exposed to different treatments. (b) Confocal microscopy images of 4T1 cells stained with calcein-AM/PI after different therapies to indicate live and dead cells. Scale bar: 50 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vitro cellular experiments on 4T1 tumor cells after different therapies. (a) Staining of 4T1 cells by DCFH-DA after different treatments to detect intracellular ROS level. Scale bar: 50 μm. Detection of (b) SO2 (Scale bar: 100 μm) and (c) •O2 − (Scale bar: 50 μm) in 4T1 cells after various treatments using DEACA and DHE as probes, respectively. (d) Measurements of mitochondrial membrane potential in 4T1 cells after different therapies. Red JC-1 aggregate indicates mitochondria with normal membrane potential, and the green JC-1 monomer means the mitochondria with a depolarized and damaged membrane (impaired mitochondria). Scale bar: 25 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vivo tumor therapeutic efficiency assessments. (a) Schematic diagram showing the establishment of 4T1 xenograft tumor model and treatment schedule. (b) Relative tumor volume curves of left tumors in different groups. (c) Average weights of the left tumors from different-treated mice on the last day. (d) Relative tumor volume curves of right tumors. (e) Average weight of the right tumors on the last day. (f) Tumors images (left and right tumors). (g) Body weight curves of tumor-bearing mice with different treatments. (h) ROS detection of left and right tumor slices by DCFH-DA for the groups exposed to various treatments (Green colour indicated ROS). Scale bar: 50 μm. The 6 groups: (1) PBS, (2) PBS + US, (3) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, (4) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, (5) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US and (6) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
H&E (Scale bar: 100 μm) and TUNEL (Scale bar: 50 μm) staining images of tumor tissues from tumor-bearing mice after various treatments. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and its application in sonodynamic-gas combined tumor therapy. (A colour version of this scheme can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: A self-assembled theranostic nanoplatform for efficient tumor sonodynamic-gas therapy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhao and Gao contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The powerful antioxidant system in tumor tissues limits the efficacy of sonodynamic therapy (SDT) by reducing the availability of the produced reactive oxygen species (ROS). To address this problem, a pH/ultrasound (US) responsive theranostic nanoplatform is elaborately constructed by self-assembly of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), benzothiazole sulfinate (BTS), Gd3+ ions and poly(lactide-co-glycolide)-b-poly(ethylene glycol) (PLGA-PEG). The NGQD component with excellent sonodynamic activity wrapped in the resulting Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can efficaciously respond to ultrasonic irradiation and cause abundant ROS generation. Importantly, in response to acidic tumor microenvironment, the loaded BTS sustainably releases SO2, which induces the increase of intracellular •O2 − level by inhibiting superoxide dismutase activity and further promotes the enrichment of ROS by reducing intracellular glutathione level and suppressing glutathione peroxidase 4 activity. The well-designed SO2 gas therapy-enhanced SDT strategy leads to high oxidative stress in tumor cells and effective inhibition of the growth of bilateral tumors with only a single intravenous injection of the nanomedicine and a subsequent single US irradiation. Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can also be employed as a good T 1-weighted magnetic resonance imaging contrast agent to guide tumor therapy. This innovative therapeutic strategy developed in our contribution may provide a new paradigm for the exploration of deep tumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the main diseases that seriously endanger human health, malignant tumors represent a significant health concern and have stimulated great efforts to develop feasible and efficient therapeutic strategies [1,2]. As a newly developed treatment modality, sonodynamic therapy (SDT) utilizes low-energy ultrasound (US) to activate the sonosensitizers accumulated in the tumors and produce reactive oxygen species (ROS), thus inducing toxicity to tumor cells and further causing antitumor effects [3–5]. SDT has emerged as a potential minimally invasive method for deep tumor therapy with high selectivity and biosafety due to its high tissue-penetration depth, highly focused properties and minimal damages to normal tissues [6,7]. Moreover, US irradiation can increase the permeability of cancer cell membrane, thereby improving the cell uptake efficiency of sonosensitizers and further enhancing the therapeutic effects [8,9]. These advantages endow SDT with attractive application prospects in tumor therapy. Traditional organic sonosensitizers such as porphyrins exhibit high ROS generation efficiency under ultrasonic irradiation [10]. Our group has employed chlorin e6 (Ce6) as a sonosensitizer to construct SDT/chemotherapy and SDT/NO gas therapy (GT) nanoplatforms for cancer treatment [11,12]. Considering that most of organic sonosensitizers suffer from several disadvantages such as high phototoxicity and insufficient sonochemical stability, researchers have turned their attention to inorganic nanosonosensitizers [13]. In recent years, Au–TiO2 [14], MnWOX [15], nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) [16] and other types of nanomaterials have been explored as sonosensitizers. Among them, NGQDs own the merits of satisfactory sonosensitive activity, high stability, no metal participant and good biocompatibility. In addition, their physical and chemical properties can be facilely and effectively regulated by selecting diverse precursors. Therefore, NGQDs can serve as high-efficiency and low-toxicity nanosonosensitizers for SDT to inhibit tumor growth.

Although the utilization of high-efficiency sonosensitizers is expected to enhance the antitumor efficacy of SDT, strong antioxidant systems within tumor tissues would reduce the availability of ROS generated by sonosensitizers. There are diverse antioxidant enzymes in tumor tissues, including glutathione (GSH) peroxidase and superoxide dismutase (SOD) [17]. There exists high level of GSH with concentrations ranging from 2 to 10 mM in tumors as well, which exhibits strong ROS scavenging effects and then amplifies the resistance of tumor cells to oxidative stress [18]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to explore a promising therapeutic method that can inhibit tumor antioxidant system, and its combination with SDT is expected to effectively break through the limitations of SDT and enhance the therapeutic effects.

SO2 is considered as a novel type of gasotransmitters after NO, CO and H2S. It can relax blood vessels and plays a significant role in regulating the function of the cardiovascular system [19]. SO2 shows excellent antibacterial properties and is expected to be used as an anti-mycobacterium agent to treat tuberculosis [20]. Recently, SO2 has emerged as a GT agent in the field of tumor treatment. It has been proposed that SO2 can convert GSH to S-sulfoglutathione, which is not a substrate for glutathione reductase, thus disrupting the normal cycle of GSH [21]. SO2 can also reduce the activity of the related antioxidant enzymes in GSH redox system [21]. In addition, SO2 can be converted into various reactive sulfur species in cells under the action of various enzymes [22]. These behaviors of SO2 induce high oxidative stress in tumor cells, causing oxidative damages to deoxyribonucleic acid, phospholipids, proteins and other biomolecules, thus killing tumor cells [23,24]. Recent studies have shown that SO2 can up-regulate the expression of pro-apoptotic proteins p53, Bax and caspase-3, and down-regulate the expression of anti-apoptotic protein bcl-2, resulting in the apoptosis of tumor cells [25,26]. In consideration of these properties of SO2, ROS-based SDT can be combined with SO2 GT to amplify oxidative stress in tumor cells by effectively destroying the tumor antioxidant system, thus killing tumor cells and effectively inhibiting tumor growth.

Citric acid (C6H8O7) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. l-alanine was received from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Methanol (CH3OH), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl acetate (CH3COOCH2CH3), petroleum ether and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were provided by General-reagent. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was obtained from Shanghai Runjie Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 2,2′-Dithiobis(benzothiazole) and N-bromosuccinimide (NBS) were purchased from Adamas-beta. Gadolinium (III) acetate hydrate (Gd(CH3CO2)3⋅xH2O) was obtained from Alfa Aesar. PLGA-PEG (10 KD, PLGA5000-b-mPEG5000) was provided by Xi'an Ruixi Biological Technology Co., Ltd. 7-Diethylaminocoumarin-3-aldehyde (DEACA) was bought from Shanghai Acmec Biochemical Co., Ltd. 9,10-Anthracenediyl-bis(methylene) dimalonic acid (ABDA) and Dihydroethidium (DHE) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Meyer (Shanghai) Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. 2′,7′-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA), total SOD assay kit with nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT), cellular glutathione peroxidase (GPX) assay kit with nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH), GSH and oxidized-glutathione (GSSG) assay kit, calcein acetoxymethyl ester (calcein-AM)/propidium iodide (PI) cell viability/cytotoxicity assay kit, mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit with 5,5′,6,6′-tetrachloro-1,1′,3,3′-tetraethylbenzimidazolocarbocyanine (JC-1) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) assay kit were supplied by Shanghai Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All chemicals are of analytical grade except for the reagents with indicated purity, and all reagents were used without additional treatments. Deionized water with a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from a field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2199). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements were performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet Avatar 370). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (BeckMan coulter DU 730). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were measured on a fluorescent spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse). FL images were obtained from a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) instrument (Leica TCS SP5). Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on an NMR spectrometer (Bruker AVANCE-400 MHz NMR). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and relaxation rates were measured on a 0.5 T MRI system (NMI 20-Analyst). In vivo T 1-weighted MRI was performed on a 1 T MRI scanner (NM42-040H–I). An US therapeutic apparatus (Chattanooga 2776) was utilized as the irradiation source for conducting sonodynamic therapy.

Briefly, 1 g of citric acid and 0.22 g of l-alanine were pyrolyzed at 200 °C until the colour of the liquid mixture turned orange. Under continuous stirring (800 rpm), the orange liquid was dispersed into 20 mL of deionized water and dialyzed in dialysis bags (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 48 h to obtain the aqueous solution of purified NGQDs.

2,2′-Dithiobis(benzothiazole) (2 g) was dissolved in the mixture of CH2Cl2 (25 mL) and methanol (25 mL) at 0 °C. N-bromosuccinimide (5.36 g) was then added into the solution within 5 min under stirring (800 rpm) and then the temperature was raised to room temperature. The reaction was monitored by thin-layer chromatography (TLC) until complete consumption of disulfide and quenched with water, and the resulted mixture was extracted with CH2Cl2. The extracted solution was washed with saturated NaHCO3 aqueous solution and deionized water three times, respectively, and dried with MgSO4. The solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure. The product was purified by column chromatography with ethyl acetate/petroleum ether (1:10) as mobile phase, obtaining a yellowish amorphous solid of methyl sulfinate (1.3 g, 57 % yield).

Methyl sulfinate (1.3 g, 6 mmol, 1 equiv) was dissolved in THF (3 mL) and H2O (3 mL), and then NaOH (0.243 g, 6 mmol, 1 equiv) was added at room temperature. The hydrolysis of methyl sulfinate was monitored by TLC. After the reaction was completed, the organic solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure and the remaining material was freeze-dried, thus yielding BTS (1.17 g, 90 % yield).

BTS (10 mg) and PLGA-PEG (20 mg) were dissolved in deionized water (1 mL) and CH2Cl2 (5 mL), respectively. The PLGA-PEG solution was then added dropwise into the BTS solution. The mixed emulsion was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, forming a water-in-oil emulsion. NGQDs (20 mg) and gadolinium (III) acetate hydrate (20 mg) were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL). This solution was added dropwise into the emulsion, and the mixture was homogeneously mixed by US. The organic solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure and the resulting aqueous solution was placed in a water bath at 50 °C for 1.5 h. The mixture was then dialyzed in dialysis bags (MWCO = 3500 Da) for 24 h to remove the unbound material. After the dialysis, the resulted self-assembled material was freeze-dried in vacuum.

For the calculations of drug loading capacity and drug loading efficiency, the absorbance values of the standard BTS solutions with various concentrations were measured at 260 nm, thus plotting standard curve of BTS. The amount of loaded BTS was quantified by subtracting the amount of unloaded BTS, which was calculated based on the absorbance of the external fluid after dialysis and the standard curve of BTS. Drug loading capacity and drug loading efficiency were calculated according to the following formulas. Drug loading capacity = mass of loaded drug/total mass of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. Drug loading efficiency = mass of loaded drug/total mass of fed drug × 100 %.

The release of Gd ions from Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG incubated under different pH conditions was assessed by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES). Three groups were assigned: (1) pH 7.4, (2) pH 6.5 and (3) pH 5.2. Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG were diluted with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (200 μg mL−1, 8 mL), packed into dialysis bags of MWCO = 3500 Da, and immersed in the corresponding buffer (16 mL) at 37 °C. The dialysate (4 mL) was taken out at various time-points (3, 6, 12 and 24 h) for ICP-AES analysis. Meanwhile, fresh buffer with an equal volume was added into the solution outside the dialysis bags.

ABDA was employed as the probe to detect the generation of 1O2 by the materials in solutions when exposed to US irradiation. The experiments were allocated into three groups: (1) BTS@PLGA-PEG + ABDA + US, (2) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + ABDA + US and (3) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + ABDA + US + NaN3. For each group, 30 μL of ABDA aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) was added to the sample solution (200 μg mL−1, 3 mL). The mixed solution was exposed to US irradiation for certain durations (power density = 1.0 W cm−2, duty cycle = 50 %, pulse frequency = 100 Hz and frequency = 1 MHz). The FL spectra of different samples at various timepoints were monitored to assess the generation capability of 1O2 (λex = 380 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of SO2 release in solutions
ce:section.ce:para: DEACA was used as an FL probe to detect the generation of SO2. A series of standard NaHSO3 solutions were prepared and the concentrations were set to 40, 60, 80, 120, 160 and 200 μM. After that, DEACA (100 μM, 100 μL) was added (the total volume of the mixture is 2 mL and the concentration is 5 μM), followed by 5 min of incubation. Finally, the FL intensities of different samples were measured by a FL spectrophotometer (λex = 390 nm, λem = 420–580 nm). As a result, standard NaHSO3 curve was plotted according to the FL intensities at λem = 483 nm.

Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG or BTS (200 μg mL−1) was incubated in PBS (pH = 7.4, 6.5 or 5.2) solutions for 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18 and 24 h. After that, DEACA (100 μM, 100 μL) was added (the total volume of the mixture is 2 mL and the concentration is 5 μM), followed by 5 min of incubation. Finally, the FL intensities of different samples were measured by a FL spectrophotometer (λex = 483 nm).

BTS (200 μg mL−1) was incubated in PBS (pH = 7.4, 6.5 or 5.2) solutions for 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18 and 24 h. After incubation, the solutions were exposed to US irradiation for 5 min (power density = 1.0 W cm−2, duty cycle = 50 %, pulse frequency = 100 Hz and frequency = 1 MHz). After that, DEACA (100 μM, 100 μL) was added (the total volume of the mixture is 2 mL and the concentration is 5 μM), followed by 5 min of incubation. Finally, the FL intensities of different samples were measured by a FL spectrophotometer (λex = 483 nm).

The MRI images and relaxation rates of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG solutions were measured on a 0.5 T MRI analyzer (NMI20-Analyst). The gradient Gd3+ concentrations in the samples were quantified by ICP-AES. The T 1 and T 2-weighted MRI images were scanned with a conventional spin-echo sequences (T 1: repetition time (TR) = 300 ms, echo time (TE) = 0.02 ms; T 2: TR = 6000 ms, TE = 300 ms).

4T1 murine breast cancer cells and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were provided by Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center. 4T1 cells were cultured with Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 containing 1 % antibiotic mixture (penicillin-streptomycin) and 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C under 5 % CO2. The medium used for incubating HUVECs was Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium and other incubation conditions were the same as those of 4T1 cells.

4T1 cells (1.0 × 106 cells) were incubated with Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG (200 μg mL−1) for 0, 2, 4, 6 and 8 h. After removing the excess material, the cells were collected and the intracellular Gd content was measured by ICP-AES to analyze the cellular uptake of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG.

The in vitro cytotoxicities of Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG against 4T1 cells and HUVECs were assessed using the standard MTT assay. The cells (1.0 × 104 per well) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated with the materials of various concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg mL−1) for 12 and 24 h, respectively. After that, the cell viability was determined by the MTT assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro SO2 release and ROS generation
ce:section.ce:para: The release of SO2 in tumor cells was detected using DEACA. Six groups were involved in the experiments, including (1) PBS, (2) US, (3) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, (4) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, (5) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US and (6) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US. After incubation with the materials (200 μg mL−1) for 6 h in 35-mm laser confocal petri dishes (2.0 × 105 per dish), the cells in Groups (2), (5) and (6) were treated with US irradiation (duty cycle = 50 %, frequency = 1 MHz, pulse frequency = 100 Hz, power density = 1.0 W cm−2) for 5 min. Subsequently, the DEACA solution (5 μM, 1 mL) was added and incubated with the cells for 30 min. Finally, the SO2 level in tumor cells (λex = 405 nm, λem = 450−510 nm) were observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM).

For intracellular •O2 − and total ROS detection, the experiment grouping (6 groups) and US irradiation conditions were the same as those described above for SO2 release observation. DHE was used to detect the intracellular •O2 − level. After various treatments, the 4T1 cells (2.0 × 105 per dish) were incubated with DHE solution (20 μM, 1 mL) for 30 min. Finally, the intracellular •O2 − levels (λex = 488 nm, λem = 580−650 nm) were evaluated by CLSM observation. The ROS generation in tumor cells was detected by DCFH-DA, which was added (10 μM, 1 mL) and incubated for 30 min with the cells treated by the materials. Finally, the ROS levels in tumor cells were assessed by CLSM after US irradiation (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500−570 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of related enzyme activities and measurement of GSH/oxidized-glutathione (GSSG) levels
ce:section.ce:para: Four groups were involved in the experiments: (1) PBS, (2) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG (134.6 μg mL−1, 1 mL), (3) BTS (65.4 μg mL−1, 1 mL) and (4) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG (200 μg mL−1, 1 mL). 4T1 cells (1.0 × 106 per well) were seeded in 6-well plates and incubated with different materials for 6 h. Then, all the cells were collected for further measurement. The SOD enzyme activities of different treatment groups were assessed using a total SOD assay kit with NBT by measuring the absorbance at 560 nm. The GPX4 enzyme activities of various groups were evaluated by a cellular glutathione peroxidase assay kit with NADPH by recording the absorbance at 340 nm. The GSH and GSSG levels of each group were compared with a GSH and GSSG assay kit by recording the absorbance at 412 nm.

In vitro therapeutic effects of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG were evaluated by the MTT assay and calcein-AM/PI staining. The experiments were divided into six groups: (1) PBS, (2) PBS + US, (3) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, (4) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, (5) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US and (6) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US. 4T1 cells (1.0 × 104 per well) were cultured in 96-well plates and then incubated with various concentrations of materials (0, 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg mL−1) for 6 h. After removing excess materials, the cells of Group (2), (5) and (6) were treated with US irradiation (duty cycle = 50 %, frequency = 1 MHz, pulse frequency = 100 Hz, power density = 1.0 W cm−2) for 5 min. The cells were further incubated for 2 h, and then the cell viability was determined by MTT assay.

For calcein-AM/PI staining, 4T1 cells (2.0 × 105 per dish) seeded in 35-mm laser confocal petri dishes were incubated with the materials (200 μg mL−1) for 6 h, washed three times with PBS, and then stained by calcein-AM/PI. The groups of (2), (5) and (6) were subjected to US irradiation for 5 min. After incubation for another 2 h, the living and dead cells were observed using CLSM (calcein-AM: λex = 488 nm, λem = 490−530 nm; PI: λex = 543 nm, λem = 590−630 nm).

The change of cellular mitochondrial membrane potential was detected using a JC-1 assay kit. The experiment grouping is the same as that for calcein-AM/PI staining. Briefly, 4T1 cells (2.0 × 105 per dish) were seeded onto CLSM-exclusive culture dishes, and then different samples (200 μg mL−1) were added and incubated with the cells for 6 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were incubated with JC-1 dye, and then groups (2), (5) and (6) were exposed to US treatment for 5 min. Finally, the cells were observed by CLSM (JC-1 momoner: λex = 488 nm, λem = 500−550 nm; JC-1 polymer: λex = 543 nm, λem = 570−610 nm).

Intracellular ATP levels were measured using an ATP assay kit. The 4T1 cells seeded in 6-well plates (1.0 × 106 per well) were treated with (1) PBS, (2) PBS + US, (3) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, (4) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, (5) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US and (6) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US. After incubation with the materials (200 μg mL−1) for 6 h, the cells of group (2), (5) and (6) were exposed to US treatment for 5 min. Finally, the lysis buffer (200 μL) was added to each well, and the lysed cells were collected for determination of intracellular ATP levels.

The hemolysis tests were divided into seven groups with PBS and H2O as the negative and positive controls, respectively. The concentration of the material was set to 50, 100, 200, 400 and 600 μg mL−1 to measure the hemolysis effect. Each group was tested three times in parallel. The blood was taken from the enucleated eyeball of the mice, centrifuged (1500 rpm) and washed with PBS until the supernatant was colorless to obtain the red blood cells (RBCs). Then the RBCs were dispersed in 12 mL of PBS. The PBS solutions (1 mL) of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG at various concentrations were mixed with 400 μL of the RBCs suspension. The mixtures were allowed to stand for 2 h. After centrifugation (1500 rpm), the absorbance of the supernatants was measured at 576 nm to calculate the hemolysis rate according to the following equation: Hemolysis rate = (Abs (experimental group) − Abs (negative control))/(Abs (positive control) − Abs (negative control)) × 100 %.

BALB/c female mice (4–5 weeks old) were purchased from Shanghai JieSiJie Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. All animal care and experimental procedures were performed in accordance with the national and local Guidelines for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (China). The animal experiments were approved by the Experimental Animal Management and Animal Welfare Ethics Committee of Shanghai Normal University. 4T1 cells (2.0 × 106 cell mL−1, 100 μL) were subcutaneously injected symmetrically into left and right hind legs of each mouse. When the tumor volume reached about 100 mm3, the in vivo imaging and tumor treatments could be performed.

In vivo T 1-weighted MRI was performed on a 1 T MRI scanner (NM42-040H–I). Tumor-bearing mice were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injecting chloral hydrate solution (10 wt%). The PBS solution of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG ([Gd] = 1.6 mg kg−1, 100 μL) was intravenously injected into the tumor-bearing mice via tail vein. MRI scanning of the mice was performed at 3, 6, 9 and 12 h after the injection with the mice before injection (0 h) as the blank control group (MRI parameters: TE = 18 ms, TR = 500 ms, slice thickness = 3 mm).

Tumor-bearing mice were randomly assigned into 6 groups (6 mice per group): (1) PBS, (2) PBS + US, (3) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, (4) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, (5) Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US and (6) Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US. During the treatment, an ultrasonic probe (2 cm2) was placed on the right tumor, and US irradiation was applied from the right tumor to the left one with the following US parameters: power density = 1 W cm−2, duty cycle = 50 %, pulse frequency = 100 Hz and frequency = 1 MHz. The right tumor surface was coated with ultrasonic couplant by 1 cm in thickness.

The material of each group (40 mg kg−1, 100 μL) was injected via tail vein on day 1, and US irradiation (10 min) was then performed on groups of (2), (5) and (6) at 6 h after injection. After US irradiation, one mouse in groups of (1), (4), (5) and (6) was randomly selected to dissect the left and right tumors for DCFH-DA staining to detect ROS level of tumor tissues. DEACA staining was performed on tumor sections of groups (1), (4) and (6) to detect the SO2 release in tumor tissues. The left and right tumors from one mouse in each group were resected for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick-end labeling (TUNEL) staining. Body weights and tumor volumes of the mice at both sites were monitored for 20 days. Tumor volume was calculated as: volume = (length × width2)/2. After 20 days of monitoring, the major organs of mice in the groups of (1), (4) and (6) were harvested for histological analysis. Blood was also taken from the eyes of mice for blood routine test and serum biochemical analysis (liver and kidney indexes).

As illustrated in Scheme 1, the sonosensitizer NGQDs were synthesized by the pyrolysis of citric acid and l-alanine [27]. TEM image (Fig. S1) shows that the as-synthesized NGQDs have a narrow lateral size distribution of about 4–5 nm (Fig. S2). High resolution TEM image of NGQDs exhibits clear lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.340 nm (Fig. S3), which can be ascribed to the characteristic (002) crystal plane of NGQDs. The typical AFM image reveals that the heights of the NGQD sheet were mainly between 0.5 and 2.5 nm (Figs. S4a and b), corresponding to 1–6 graphene layers. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S5) displays a characteristic absorption peak at 204 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds, indicative of extended conjugation structures of NGQDs. In addition, the absorption band in the range of 300–400 nm is assigned to n-π* transitions of CO or C–O bonds [28]. Raman spectrum of NGQDs exhibited typical D (sp3-hybrid) and G (sp2-hybrid) bands around 1372 and 1553 cm−1, respectively, demonstrating the formation of graphite structures (Fig. S6). The intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) is about 0.95, manifesting that NGQDs possess a high degree of graphitization. XPS spectrum in Fig. S7a illustrates that the elements of C, N and O exist in NGQDs. Three peaks exhibited in high-resolution C 1s XPS profiles at 285.1, 286.3 and 288.5 eV are assigned to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O and CO (Fig. S7b), respectively [27]. High-resolution N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. S7c) exhibit two peaks at 400.9 and 400.6 eV, which refer to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively [27]. Additionally, high resolution O 1s spectra indicate the presence of CO and C–O (Fig. S7d). Fig. S8 shows the FL spectra of NGQDs at different excitation wavelengths, and the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of NGQDs are 345 and 430 nm, respectively. Next, the SO2 donor BTS was synthesized according to the literature [29]. The 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra (Figs. S9a and b) confirm the successful preparation of BTS [29]. Finally, by means of double emulsion-solvent evaporation method, Gd3+ ions, NGQDs and BTS molecules were co-assembled with the aid of amphiphilic polymer PLGA-PEG, thus yielding the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG nanocomposite. The loading capacity and loading efficiency of BTS in Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG were calculated based on the quantitative analysis of the unloaded BTS by its absorbance at 260 nm (Figs. S10a and b). With the increase of BTS feeding mass, the loading capacity of BTS was raised, while its loading efficiency descended (Fig. S10c). When the feeding mass of BTS was 10 mg, the loading capacity of BTS in Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG was calculated to be 178 mg g−1 and its corresponding loading efficiency is 32.7 % (Figs. S10c and d). Taking into account the loading capacity and loading efficiency, we chose 10 mg of BTS to prepare the final nanomedicine.

The TEM image of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG (Fig. 1 a) illustrates that it mainly presents a spherical morphology, but there is a certain degree of adhesion. It can be clearly observed that the NGQDs are evenly distributed in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG nanoparticles. DLS data (Fig. S11) reveal that Z-average hydrodynamic size of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG is about 124 nm. This may be due to the relaxation of the hydrophilic PEG chains in aqueous solution and partial adhesion of the self-assembled nanoparticles. Zeta potentials of NGQDs, NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG were measured as −20.6, −9.99, −8.64 and −5.03 mV, respectively (Fig. 1b). Evident change of Zeta potentials in different systems supports the successful synthesis of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. UV–vis absorption spectra of NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG (Fig. 1c) show multiple absorption peaks at 216, 250, 284, 294 and 320 nm. The first four peaks are mainly attributed to BTS, while the last one comes from NGQDs. Compared with free NGQDs and BTS, these peaks all have a certain blue shift after the formation of assembly materials. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, the characteristic bands at 1383 and 1592 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG refer to the C–N stretching vibrations and N–H bending vibrations, respectively, clearly indicating the existence of NGQDs [30,31]. The absorption peaks at 1057 and 1318 cm−1 were assigned to the S–O and SO stretching vibrations, respectively, further confirming the successful loading of BTS [29]. In addition, the bands at 2800∼3050 cm−1 were due to the C–H stretching vibrations, and other characteristic bands including 1751, 1187 and 1104 cm−1 were attributed to CO and C–O stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of PLGA-PEG [32]. The fed Gd3+ ions may coordinate with oxygen-containing functional groups on NGQDs and PLGA-PEG. The XPS survey spectrum exhibited in Fig. 1e shows that the elements of C, N, O, Gd and S exist in Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. The atomic number ratio of C, N, O, Gd and S is 60.17 %: 8.75 %: 23.25 %: 1.34 %: 6.49 %. Gd 4d region was fitted with four peaks (Fig. 1f), indicating a multiplet structure [33], which may be due to the coordination of Gd3+ ions with oxygen-containing functional groups on NGQDs and PLGA-PEG. The content of Gd3+ in Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG was also determined to be 0.48 mmol g−1 by ICP-AES. Hydrodynamic size monitoring by DLS analysis for 7 days and photographs of different dispersions after 7 days of standing (Figs. S12a and b) reveal that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can be stably dispersed in PBS, RPMI 1640 and FBS, indicating its excellent dispersibility in physiological solutions.

The Gd3+ release behavior of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG at different pH values was also investigated. Slow release of Gd ions (Fig. S13) at pH 7.4 manifests that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can exist stably under neutral pH condition. More Gd ions were released with the increase in solution acidity, and the maximum release reached nearly 17 % at pH 5.2, which may be due to the decreased binding ability of Gd3+ ions to the oxygen-containing functional groups on NGQDs and PLGA-PEG in acidic environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS production and SO2 generation measurement
ce:section.ce:para: As newly-developed highly efficient nanosonosensitizers [16], NGQDs have been found to exhibit excellent singlet oxygen (1O2) production capacity under US stimulation. To evaluate the sonodynamic performance of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG involving NGQDs, ABDA was used as a probe to detect US-triggered 1O2 production capacity of the nanomedicine. ABDA can react with 1O2 to form an endoperoxide, thus leading to the decrease in FL intensity (Figs. S14a and b, Fig. S16a) [34]. When exposure to only US irradiation or BTS@PLGA-PEG + US, ABDA did not show apparent FL intensity change (Fig. S14c, Fig. S15a, Fig. S16b). However, in the presence of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, the FL intensity decreased continuously with increasing US time (Fig. 2 a; Fig. S16b) within 60 min, suggesting the persistent production of 1O2. After adding NaN3, an 1O2 quenching agent, the FL intensity of the indicator did not decrease with prolonged US time (Fig. S15b, Fig. S16b) owing to the prior capture of 1O2 by NaN3. These studies revealed that NGQDs still retain excellent sonodynamic activity (Fig. S14, Fig. S16a) after being wrapped in Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG. The satisfied 1O2 generation ability of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG under US irradiation lays an important foundation for its application in SDT.

Since the C–S bonds of BTS can be broken under acidic conditions and then SO2 is released [35], DEACA was used as an FL probe to monitor SO2 release from donor BTS at different pH values. The released SO2 can exist in the form of HSO3 − in aqueous solutions and undergo a nucleophilic addition reaction with DEACA to significantly enhance the FL emission (Figs. S17a and b). As illustrated in Figs. S17c–e, free BTS exhibited a pH-dependent SO2 release behavior with more SO2 being released in the acidic environment. The SO2 release behavior with or without US irradiation was also investigated (Fig. S18, Fig. S19). It is concluded that US irradiation could not promote the SO2 release from BTS. After the SO2 donor was assembled in Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, pH-dependent SO2 release behavior was observed again. At pH = 7.4, the FL intensity of DEACA was nearly unchanged with prolonged incubation time (Fig. S20). In contrast, at pH 6.5 or 5.2, the FL intensity was raised with increasing incubation time, and the trend was more apparent for the latter one (Fig. 2b and c). After 24 h of incubation at pH 5.2, the concentration of released SO2 was as high as 100.7 μM. The changes in the release of SO2 at different pH values (Fig. 2d) indicate that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can effectively respond to acidic environment and sustainably release SO2 for tumor GT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo MRI
ce:section.ce:para: The MRI contrast properties of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG in aqueous solutions were investigated by measuring the relaxation time and corresponding T 1/T 2 weighted images of the material with different Gd3+ concentrations. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the longitudinal (T 1) relaxation rate r 1 and horizontal (T 2) relaxation rate r 2 of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG were determined to be 13.11 and 15.22 mM−1 s−1, respectively. With increasing concentration of Gd3+, Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG exhibited concentration-dependent signal enhancement in T 1-weighted MRI images but nearly no enhancement in T 2-weighted images (Fig. 3b). The r 2 /r 1 ratio of 1.16 also suggests that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can be utilized as a T 1-weighted MRI contrast agent. The measured r 1 value of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG was found to be noticeably greater than that of gadolinium-based chelating agents reported in the literatures [36,37]. The chelation of Gd3+ ions with oxygen-containing groups on NGQDs and PLGA-PEG led to their increased local concentration and decreased rotation speed, which may be the reason for the significant increase in r 1 value of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG.

Considering that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG exhibited good T 1-weighted contrast performance in solutions, in vivo MRI was further investigated on 4T1 tumor-bearing mice by intravenous injection of the nanocomposite. The MRI signal intensity at the tumor site increased with time, which could be attributed to the gradual accumulation of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG in tumor tissues via the enhanced permeation and retention effect. The unilateral tumor site exhibited the maximum MRI contrast enhancement at 6 h after injection of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG (Fig. 3c,e). Additionally, at 6 h postinjection, the maximum MRI contrast signals were also detected at bilateral tumor sites (Fig. 3d,f,g), suggesting that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG could be utilized as an effective T 1-weighted MRI contrast agent for in vivo imaging-guided tumor therapy.

An important characteristic required for antitumor nanomedicines is that they are not significantly toxic to normal cells. Herein, HUVECs were used as a cell model to evaluate the cytotoxicity of Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG on normal cells by the standard MTT assay. As illustrated in Fig. S21a, Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG exhibited no significant toxicity effects on HUVECs at the tested doses. Even when loaded with BTS, Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG-treated HUVECs still had a survival rate of more than 80 % (Fig. S21b), indicating very low cytotoxicity towards normal cells. The Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG without BTS also showed negligible toxicity on 4T1 cells (Fig. S22a). In contrast, Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG caused a significant decrease in the survival rate of the tumor cells (Fig. S22b), mainly due to sustained release of SO2. The low toxicity of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG to normal cells but obvious toxicity to tumor cells is of great significance for its applications in tumor therapy.

In addition to no obvious toxicity to vascular endothelial cells, Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG also showed a very low hemolysis ratio, which is less than 1 % at a concentration up to 600 μg mL−1 (Fig. S23). The above in vitro evaluation results demonstrate that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG exhibits desirable biocompatibility and biosafety for further in vitro and in vivo antitumor therapy research.

Before in vitro anticancer treatment, the optimal cellular uptake time of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG was explored to identify the time point of ultrasonic irradiation. As can be seen from Fig. S24, Gd3+ content in 4T1 cells reached the maximum when incubated for 6 h, indicating the maximum uptake amount of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG by the cells at this time point. Therefore, 6 h was chosen as the optimal incubation time of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG with 4T1 cells for following in vitro combination therapy.

The viability of 4T1 cells under different treatment conditions was then investigated by MTT assay to evaluate the in vitro therapeutic effects of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG under US irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 a, only US irradiation or Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG treatment exhibited no significant toxic effects on the tumor cells. In the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG (Fig. 4a) or Gd/BTS@PLGA-PEG (Fig. S22c) group, there was a marked decline in cell viability, which dropped to about 60 % or 70 % at 400 μg mL−1, owing to the GT effect of the released SO2. In comparison with the Gd/BTS@PLGA-PEG group, the cell viability of the Gd/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group dropped to ∼59 % (Fig. S22c), probably due to the released SO2 and the additional cavitation effect of US. Meanwhile, the Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US group showed significantly enhanced therapeutic effect with the cell viability as low as ∼32 %, mainly due to the cell damage caused by ROS generated under US irradiation. Notably, for the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group, the cell viability was reduced to only ∼14 % compared with the single treatment groups, indicating that the combination of SDT and SO2 has a powerful killing effect on tumor cells.

Furthermore, for direct visualization, the cell groups after different treatments were co-stained with calcein-AM and PI. Viable and dead cells were labeled with green and red FL, respectively (Fig. 4b). No significant red FL was observed in the groups of US and Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, demonstrating that only US or Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG caused negligible damages to 4T1 cells. By comparison, increased red FL was found in tumor cells treated with Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG containing SO2 donors, suggesting that only single SO2 GT played a limited role in killing tumor cells. The more pronounced red FL in the Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US group than that in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG group illustrates more efficient tumor cell killing effect of SDT arising from NGQDs. It is worth noting that the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group exhibited the most significant red FL, indicating the best therapeutic efficacy, which was consistent with the MTT results described above. Therefore, the ingenious combination of NGQDs-based SDT and SO2-based GT would significantly improve the antitumor ability of the nanomedicine and achieve the expected therapeutic effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro mechanism for combined SDT/SO2 GT
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the mechanism of in vitro combined SDT/SO2 GT using Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, related intracellular detection experiments were carried out. DCFH-DA, which could react with ROS to form 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein with green FL, was utilized as the FL probe to detect ROS generation within 4T1 cells. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, weak green FL was observed in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG group, mainly owing to the increase of intracellular •O2 − level induced by the released SO2. In contrast, strong green FL was detected in both Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US groups, indicating abundant ROS generation upon US irradiation. The significantly stronger green FL in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group (Fig. S25) means that much more ROS were produced, which is attributed to the synergic effect of the sonodynamic property of NGQDs and the SO2 released by the donor. The highest oxidative stress in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group resulted in the lowest cell survival rate.

The level of SO2 released in 4T1 cells was then detected with DEACA by measuring the intensity of blue FL arising from the combination of DEACA and SO2. Compared with the other four groups, the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US groups showed significant blue FL (Fig. 5b), and the FL was stronger in the latter group (Fig. S26). These results indicated that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG could still effectively release SO2 in the cells after internalization and US irradiation was conducive to intracellular production of SO2. This also revealed that the ROS produced by ultrasonic excitation of NGQDs had no obvious oxidation effect on the released SO2 and its related derivatives (H2SO3, HSO3 − and SO3 2−) in aqueous solutions, which was conducive to improving the effect of combination therapy.

Next, the effect of released SO2 on enhancing oxidative stress in the tumor cells was investigated. Considering that SO2 can enhance intracellular •O2 − level by inhibiting SOD activity [38,39], the •O2 − level of 4T1 cells after incubation with Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG was investigated using DHE, a commonly used FL probe for the detection of superoxides produced in cells [38]. As shown in Fig. 5c, no red FL was observed in the groups that did not involve SO2 donors. In contrast, the cells treated with Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG displayed significant red FL and stronger red FL was also observed in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group (Fig. S27), confirming enhanced intracellular •O2 − level by the released SO2. In order to explore the mechanism of increasing •O2 − level caused by SO2, the SOD activities in different treatment groups were measured. As illustrated in Fig. S28, both the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and BTS groups containing SO2 donor exhibited significantly weaker SOD activities than the control and Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG groups, verifying that the released SO2 inhibited the SOD activity and thus enhanced intracellular •O2 − level. SO2 can also significantly decrease GPX4 activity, and an important reason may be due to the consumption of GSH [39–41]. In vitro GSH depletion capacity of SO2 was then investigated. 4T1 cells were incubated with Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG, BTS and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, respectively. The corresponding GSH contents within 4T1 cells were measured to be 95.33 ± 2.29 %, 63.17 ± 1.70 % and 60.05 ± 2.71 % (Fig. S29a), respectively, suggesting a remarkable decline in intracellular GSH level in the groups containing the SO2 donor. Additionally, the GSSG content within 4T1 cells treated with BTS or Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG was nearly 5 times of that treated with Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG (Fig. S29b). Therefore, the released SO2 can efficiently deplete intracellular GSH. The GPX4 activities were also assessed after various treatments and the results were shown in Fig. S30. Significantly lower GPX4 activities were found in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and BTS groups when compared with the control and Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG groups, confirming that SO2 released by BTS can effectively suppress GPX4 activity. These favorable properties of intracellular released SO2, including the reduction in GSH levels and the inhibition of SOD and GPX4 activities, lead to the decline of cellular antioxidant capacity. Therefore, the rational combination of NGQDs-based SDT and SO2-based GT can significantly cause high oxidative stress in tumor cells.

Moreover, the mitochondrial integrity was examined by JC-1 staining, as the cell apoptosis is highly related to the mitochondrial dysfunction [42]. No green FL was observed in the control group, while only weak green FL was detected in the US group (Fig. 5d), indicating that US irradiation caused no significant damage to the mitochondrial membrane. Significant green FL was observed in both Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US groups, which was caused by the released SO2 and SDT, respectively. The latter group showed stronger green FL, implying a more significant reduction in mitochondrial membrane potential of 4T1 cells caused by SDT. The combination of SDT and SO2 GT further reduces mitochondrial membrane potential of the tumor cells, thus effectively disrupting the mitochondrial function. During the apoptosis process, the ATP level decreases simultaneously with the decrease of mitochondrial membrane potential [43], so the intracellular ATP levels were also detected (Fig. S31). The Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG, Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US groups exhibited about 26 %, 44 % and 63 % decrease in ATP levels, respectively, while US treatment alone did not significantly influence the intracellular ATP levels. The lowest ATP level in Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group was due to the high inhibition of ATP production by the combination of SDT and SO2 GT. Therefore, the high oxidative stress induced by Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG plus US irradiation can substantially inhibit mitochondrial respiration and ATP synthesis of tumor cells, thus effectively inhibiting tumor cell survival.

Finally, in vivo antitumor efficacy of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG under US irradiation was assessed by employing the mouse 4T1 breast tumor model (Fig. 6 a). Tumor-bearing mice were randomly assigned into 6 groups (Fig. S32). During the treatment, PBS, Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG or Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG was intravenously injected on day 1, and then US irradiation was performed on the right tumor at 6 h after injection with the direction from the right tumor to the left one. The dosage of the nanomedicines was set to be 40 mg kg−1 for consideration of safety and pharmacodynamic effects. Compared with the PBS group, the tumor growth trend of the PBS + US group was slightly decreased (Fig. 6b), suggesting certain tumor inhibition efficacy of US irradiation. Limited tumor suppression rates (51.0 % for left site and 48.8 % for right site) in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG group were found on the last day (Fig. 6c), indicating unsatisfactory therapeutic effect of only SO2 GT. The Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US group showed better antitumor effects, with significant tumor suppression of both sites (67.3 % for left site and 78.3 % for right site) (Fig. 6c–f). The inhibition trend of the left tumor was only slightly lower than that of the right tumor, mainly due to the good tissue penetration ability of US used in SDT. Importantly, the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group showed much better antitumor effects than the above groups, with tumor suppression rates of both sites as high as 84.0 % (for left tumor) and 94.9 % (for right tumor), due to the effective combination of SDT and SO2 GT. The excellent in vivo tumor therapeutic efficacy of the combined SDT/SO2 GT and the strong penetrating ability of US irradiation endow Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG with a good application prospect in the treatment of deep tumors.

To evaluate in vivo ROS generation in different treatment groups, tumor sections from relevant groups were stained with the ROS probe DCFH-DA. Compared with the PBS and Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG groups, a large amount of ROS was generated in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG group (Fig. 6h, Fig. S33), indicating that the nanomedicine arriving at the tumor site could still effectively release SO2 to induce ROS accumulation. Tumor tissues in the Gd-NGQDs@PLGA-PEG + US group showed strong green FL, which was due to the high ROS production caused by the good sonodynamic properties of NGQDs. Expectedly, strongest green FL was observed in the mice treated with both Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and US (Fig. 6h, Fig. S33), which proves that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG accumulated in tumor tissues exhibited excellent sonodynamic activity and SO2 release performance under US irradiation, thus leading to the generation and enrichment of the maximum amount of ROS for highly effective tumor therapy.

The release of SO2 from Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG in the tumors was also assessed using DEACA as a probe. Compared with the PBS group (Fig. S34 and Fig. S35), bright green FL was observed in tumor tissues (both sites) of the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US groups, while no obvious green FL was detected in the liver and spleen, suggesting that Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG accumulated at the tumor sites could effectively generate SO2 in response to mild acidic tumor microenvironment. This can reduce the toxic side effects of the nanomedicines on normal tissues and organs. DEACA staining of left and right tumor tissue sections also showed that there was no significant difference in the released SO2 levels between the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG and Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US groups, which further indicated that ROS produced by sonodynamic therapy did not significantly oxidize SO2 and its related derivatives (H2SO3, HSO3 − and SO3 2−), which may be related to the complex tumor microenvironment.

Futhermore, H&E staining and TUNEL staining of tumor sections from different treatment groups were conducted to demonstrate excellent tumor suppression effects of the combined therapy. Compared with other groups, both the left and right tumor tissues in the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG + US group suffered the most serious destruction and showed the highest apoptosis degree of tumor cells (Fig. 7 ).

Finally, the safety of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG in tumor therapy was preliminarily evaluated. No significant bodyweight loss was observed in each group (Fig. 6g), implying a negligible adverse effect of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG on mouse growth. Besides, the mice were euthanized at the end of treatment follow-up, and the major organs and blood were collected for pathological and hematological analysis. H&E staining images of major normal organs presented negligible pathological abnormalities (Fig. S36a). Meanwhile, there were no abnormal changes in blood routine and liver and kidney indexs of different treatment groups (Figs. S36b and c), manifesting the satisfied biocompatibility and therapeutic biosafety of Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG.

In summary, the Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG theranostic nanoplatform constructed here has shown the following merits for tumor therapy. Firstly, under US irradiation, Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can induce the production of a large number of ROS for SDT, which is attributed to the outstanding sonodynamic activity of NGQDs. Secondly, Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG can respond to the acidic tumor microenvironment and continuously release SO2, which would significantly reduce the level of intracellular GSH and suppress the enzyme activities of SOD and GPX4. The cooperation of SDT with SO2-based GT causes significantly high oxidative stress in tumor cells and effectively inhibits tumor growth. Thirdly, Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG shows good T 1-weighted MRI performances at tumor sites and can serve as an efficient MRI contrast agent to guide tumor treatment. Lastly, Gd-NGQDs/BTS@PLGA-PEG exhibits high biocompatibility and favorable therapeutic biosafety. This work provides a model reference for the effective combination of SDT and GT, and our established nanoplatform for combined SDT/SO2 GT offers a new strategy for deep tumor therapy.
